results.title: Immersive sound in cinema: THX
results.brief_description:
When Star Wars hit cinemas in the late 1970s and 80s, sound engineer Tomlinson Holman noticed a problem: the rich, detailed audio created during production wasn’t being heard properly in theatres. His solution was THX – a new sound standard that ensured films played exactly as filmmakers intended.
When Return of the Jedi (1983) premiered, only THX-certified cinemas could show it. Audiences were greeted by the now-iconic ‘Deep Note’ – a rising wave of tones swelling into a mighty D major chord. It shook the room and set the stage for the galaxy far, far away – as well as a new level of excellence in cinema sound.
Fun fact: THX was named after George Lucas’s first film, THX 1138 (1971).

constellations_primary.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_primary.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

results.title: Cinerama
results.brief_description:
By the early 1950s, television was keeping people at home, and cinemas needed to offer something special. Sound became key to creating an experience that couldn’t be matched in the living room.
In 1952, This Is Cinerama wowed New York audiences with a huge curved screen and a new multi-channel sound system developed by Hazard E Reeves. Instead of a single speaker, Cinerama used seven – five behind the screen and two behind the audience – wrapping viewers in sound.
This early form of surround sound transformed movie-going, setting the stage for the immersive audio experiences we expect in cinemas today.

constellations_other.name: Big screen dreams
constellations_other.description: With groundbreaking technology and a unique cinema experience, today IMAX theatres are not only loved by audiences but championed by pioneering filmmakers like Christopher Nolan and James Cameron. Yet the route to get there was an unconventional one.

results.title: Celeste
results.brief_description: Celeste (2018) is a game about continuing onwards, even when things are tough. Created by Noel Berry and Maddy Thorson just before Thorson’s gender transition, the game is a metaphor for a young woman facing struggles with mental health and identity, represented as one great task: climbing a mountain.

constellations_primary.name: Keeping the pace
constellations_primary.description: Whether speedrunning or in slow motion, explore how running builds tension, emotion and excitement on screen.

results.description:
Celeste (2018) is a game about continuing onwards, even when things are tough. Created by Noel Berry and Maddy Thorson just before Thorson’s gender transition, the game is a metaphor for a young woman facing struggles with mental health and identity, represented as one great task: climbing a mountain.

The game’s precise movement and challenging levels have drawn a passionate community of players who race through the mountain in speedruns, sharing strategies and competing for the fastest climbs. Celeste celebrates persistence, reflection, and skill, showing how a game can tell a deeply personal story while connecting people through play.
description_for_label:

Celeste celebrates persistence, reflection, and skill, showing how a game can tell a deeply personal story while connecting people through play.

results.title: Forced into images
results.material_description: Single channel moving image, colour, silent
results.brief_description: In Destiny Deacon’s photographic series Forced into Images (a title taken from a poem by Alice Walker), a dramatic narrative unfolds that follows a girl’s troubled life from birth to womanhood. Deacon’s work often explores cultural stereotyping, domestic violence and the status of Indigenous women.

constellations_other.name: Women in charge
constellations_other.description: Across documentary, film, TV, and visual art, Aboriginal women have led how they will be represented on-screen.

results.description: DESTINY DEACON has shown in exhibitions including the 2001 Yokohama Triennale, Das Lied von der Erde at the Museum Fridericianum in Kassel, the 2000 Biennale of Sydney and the 1995 Havana Biennial and the Yokohoma triennial. Using people and objects close to her - friends and family, dolls and other toys - the artist creates strange, unsettling images that address some of the most difficult aspects of contemporary Australian life. Cultural stereotyping, domestic violence and the status of Aboriginal women all inform Deacon’s photography, and her gift for storytelling and biting sense of humour provide her work with a compelling edge. With her photographic series Forced into Images (a title taken from a poem by Alice Walker), a dramatic narrative unfolds, following a girl’s troubled life from birth to womanhood.
description_for_label: DESTINY DEACON has shown in exhibitions including the 2001 Yokohama Triennale, Das Lied von der Erde at the Museum Fridericianum in Kassel, the 2000 Biennale of Sydney and the 1995 Havana Biennial and the Yokohoma triennial. Using people and objects close to her - friends and family, dolls and other toys - the artist creates strange, unsettling images that address some of the most difficult aspects of contemporary Australian life. Cultural stereotyping, domestic violence and the status of Aboriginal women all inform Deacon’s photography, and her gift for storytelling and biting sense of humour provide her work with a compelling edge. With her photographic series Forced into Images (a title taken from a poem by Alice Walker), a dramatic narrative unfolds, following a girl’s troubled life from birth to womanhood.

results.title: Indigenous Voices on the Screen with Professor Gary Foley
results.brief_description: Gumbainggir activist, academic, writer and actor, Professor Gary Foley charts the intersection of Indigenous activism and the world of film and TV in this short film by Yaegl director, producer and storyteller Pauline Clague.

constellations_primary.name: Black Power through film and TV
constellations_primary.description: The Black Power movement of the late 1960s and early 70s was “the most exciting and dynamic era in modern Indigenous history” according to Aboriginal activist Professor Gary Foley, who gained widespread attention for protests during the time and later leveraged film and TV to fight for Indigenous rights.

results.title_for_label: Indigenous Voices on the Screen with Professor Gary Foley
results.description:
Gumbainggir activist, academic, writer and actor, Professor Gary Foley charts the intersection of Indigenous activism and the world of film and TV in this short film by Yaegl director, producer and storyteller Pauline Clague. Through interviews, archival footage and scenes from film and TV, Foley explores the Black Power movement and the period during the 1970s and 80s when First Peoples began taking control of their representation in Australian screen culture, as well as how the moving image has been used to advocate for Indigenous Rights.

“It has been a journey recording Gary about the early years of film. How do you fit the wealth of information into an eight-minute space? I have had the privilege of recording many of the Indigenous trailblazers of the early years of television and film and know how important it is for us to remember those who made a mark on our landscape for us to dream, to make the films and stories we do now. As a person who loves their craft, I hope this gives you a glimpse of the power of our voices on-screen.” – Pauline Clague
description_for_label:
Gumbainggir activist, academic, writer and actor, Professor Gary Foley charts the intersection of Indigenous activism and the world of film and TV in this short film by Yaegl director, producer and storyteller Pauline Clague. Through interviews, archival footage and scenes from film and TV, Foley explores the Black Power movement and the period during the 1970s and 80s when First Peoples began taking control of their representation in Australian screen culture, as well as how the moving image has been used to advocate for Indigenous Rights.

“It has been a journey recording Gary about the early years of film. How do you fit the wealth of information into an eight-minute space? I have had the privilege of recording many of the Indigenous trailblazers of the early years of television and film and know how important it is for us to remember those who made a mark on our landscape for us to dream, to make the films and stories we do now. As a person who loves their craft, I hope this gives you a glimpse of the power of our voices on-screen.” – Pauline Clague

results.title: Daybill for the American release of Jedda
results.credit_line: Courtesy the National Film and Sound Archive of Australia.
results.brief_description: Jedda represented a lot of historic firsts for the Australian moving image, not just in the story it was telling but how that story was told. The first fully funded Australian narrative feature to be shot in colour in the country, it used a colour process known as the Belgian Gevacolor so it was possible to shoot on location among the Australian landscape.
results.description: Jedda represented a lot of historic firsts for the Australian moving image, not just in the story it was telling but how that story was told. The first fully funded Australian narrative feature to be shot in colour in the country, it used a colour process known as the Belgian Gevacolor so it was possible to shoot on location among the Australian landscape.

results.title: National Portable Video Camera WV-3085
results.brief_description: The Portapak system is a handheld video camera with separate reel-to-reel video recorder. It enabled videos to be captured and played back more quickly while also cutting down on the cost, which led to the birth of video art, guerrilla television and activism.

constellations_primary.name: Access to resistance
constellations_primary.description: This Constellation explores how access to new recording devices has inspired guerilla filmmaking and protest, helping challenge cultural, societal and political norms.

results.description: The Portapak system consists of a handheld video camera with a separate reel-to-reel video recorder in a portable pack that could be carried over the shoulder. The style was first released by Sony in 1967 and was an important development in camera technology, enabling a new level of portability with both the camera and recorder battery-powered and self-contained. It enabled videos to be captured and played back more quickly while also cutting down on the cost, which led to the birth of video art, guerrilla television and activism. In the 1970s, the Portapak was popular with video artists and alternative media collectives. In Australia, Open Channel and the Paddington Video Access Centre used Portapaks to create alternative broadcast channels, paving the way for community television.
description_for_label: The Portapak system consists of a handheld video camera with a separate reel-to-reel video recorder in a portable pack that could be carried over the shoulder. The style was first released by Sony in 1967 and was an important development in camera technology, enabling a new level of portability with both the camera and recorder battery-powered and self-contained. It enabled videos to be captured and played back more quickly while also cutting down on the cost, which led to the birth of video art, guerrilla television and activism. In the 1970s, the Portapak was popular with video artists and alternative media collectives. In Australia, Open Channel and the Paddington Video Access Centre used Portapaks to create alternative broadcast channels, paving the way for community television.

results.title: Cartoon connections
results.brief_description: The Simpsons and Dragon’s Lair arcade cabinets symbolise how cartoons, animation techniques and videogames have enjoyed a symbiotic relationship throughout their development.

constellations_primary.name: Cartoon classics and criticism
constellations_primary.description: A whole cavalcade of cartoons have successfully made the leap into arcades and onto consoles, but the relationship between cinema and videogames is far more likely to fizzle out.

results.description:
The Simpsons had only been on-air for one season when Konami released this side-scrolling brawler in 1991. It wasn’t that Konami knew how popular The Simpsons would become, they just already had the blueprints for the game. Two years earlier they’d developed the Teenage Mutant Nina Turtles arcade game and applied almost identical mechanics, play style and combat to The Simpsons. What set The Simpsons apart was the accurate voice renditions of Springfield’s favourite cartoon family, with the show’s main cast voicing the game’s characters.

Dragon’s Lair looks more like a cartoon than a videogame. That’s because it was animated by Don Bluth, the legend behind The Land Before Time (1988), and its LaserDisc technology allowed for dramatically better graphics and sound than other games of the era. It was advertised as the first truly 3D videogame, but maybe should’ve been advertised as the hardest. The timed button/stick gameplay was deliberately designed to eat up as many coins as possible, even though it was already twice as expensive as typical games.

What set The Simpsons apart was the accurate voice renditions of Springfield’s favourite cartoon family, with the show’s main cast voicing the game’s characters.

Dragon’s Lair looks more like a cartoon than a videogame. That’s because it was animated by Don Bluth, the legend behind The Land Before Time (1988), and its LaserDisc technology allowed for dramatically better graphics and sound than other games of the era.
